title: ACLS – Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA)
description: A patient develops PEA cardiac arrest. Practise recognising organised electrical activity without a pulse, applying the non-shockable algorithm, and systematically identifying and treating the reversible causes that underlie PEA.
learningObjectives: Define PEA and distinguish it from a pulse-generating rhythm
Use the 5H/5T framework to identify treatable causes of PEA
Initiate targeted therapy for the most likely reversible cause
Understand that a narrow-complex PEA suggests obstructive causes

states.prompt: A 52-year-old female with known malignancy develops sudden unresponsiveness during a clinic visit. The monitor shows organised narrow-complex complexes at 80 bpm. NO carotid pulse is palpable. What is this rhythm?

options.label: Normal sinus rhythm with haemodynamic instability

explanations: Correct! PEA is defined as organised electrical activity on the monitor WITHOUT a palpable pulse. It requires the non-shockable arrest algorithm.
Haemodynamic instability still requires a pulse to be a perfusing rhythm. No pulse = cardiac arrest regardless of ECG pattern.
Asystole shows no discernible complexes. This patient has organised narrow complexes.
VFib is a chaotic waveform. This is organised electrical activity.

states.prompt: PEA confirmed. IV access is obtained. What is the immediate treatment?

options.label: High-quality CPR and epinephrine 1 mg IV immediately
Defibrillate – narrow complex may respond to shock
Give calcium chloride 1 g IV as first-line for PEA

explanations: Correct! PEA follows the non-shockable algorithm: CPR + epinephrine q3–5 min + search for reversible causes.
Defibrillation is not indicated for organised PEA – the electrical system is producing complexes; the problem is mechanical (no cardiac output).
Calcium chloride is only indicated for PEA secondary to hyperkalaemia or calcium-channel-blocker toxicity — not empirically for all PEA.
Transcutaneous pacing is not recommended for PEA — the heart has intrinsic electrical activity; the problem is mechanical, not conduction.

checklistItems.description: Correctly initiates CPR and epinephrine for PEA

states.prompt: The PEA is narrow-complex. This patient has a known malignancy and you notice neck vein distension and distant heart sounds. Which diagnosis does narrow-complex PEA + these findings MOST suggest?
explanations: Correct! Beck's Triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds) in a malignancy patient strongly suggests cardiac tamponade. Narrow-complex PEA with JVD points to obstructive causes.
Tension pneumothorax also causes JVD but presents with absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation. Heart sounds remain audible.
Massive PE is possible (especially with malignancy) but muffled heart sounds and Beck's Triad point more toward tamponade. PE typically shows S1Q3T3 on ECG.
Profound hypovolaemia causes flat neck veins, not JVD.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies cardiac tamponade from clinical clues

states.prompt: Cardiac tamponade is suspected. Ultrasound confirms large pericardial effusion with RV collapse. What is the definitive emergent treatment?

options.label: Aggressive IV fluid bolus to overcome tamponade

explanations: Correct! Needle pericardiocentesis (or surgical drainage in the operating room) is the only definitive treatment for tamponade-related PEA.
IV fluids can temporarily increase preload but are only a bridge — they do not relieve the tamponade. Pericardiocentesis is required.
Steroids address inflammation but have no role in acute haemodynamic tamponade.
Fibrinolytics are for PE, not tamponade. They would be dangerous here — increasing haemorrhagic risk into the pericardium.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies and performs pericardiocentesis for tamponade PEA

states.prompt: After pericardiocentesis relieving 400 mL of bloody fluid, a strong carotid pulse returns. BP 95/60. The patient regains consciousness. What is the most critical next step?

options.label: Urgent cardiothoracic surgery/oncology referral and ICU admission for ongoing drainage
Repeat pericardiocentesis in 4 hours as a matter of routine
Observe in the clinic recovery bay for 2 hours
Start high-dose steroids to prevent re-accumulation

explanations: Correct! Malignant pericardial effusion will re-accumulate. The patient needs surgical drainage, pericardial window, or oncological treatment, plus ICU monitoring.
Routine repeat pericardiocentesis at a fixed interval is not appropriate — ongoing drainage (drain in situ) or surgical management is required.
Discharge to observation is inappropriate after PEA cardiac arrest. ICU-level monitoring is required.
Steroids reduce pericarditis-related effusions but malignant effusions require oncological or surgical management.

checklistItems.description: Plans definitive management (surgical drainage + ICU) post-ROSC from tamponade

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• PEA = organised ECG activity WITHOUT a pulse; non-shockable algorithm
• Narrow-complex PEA + JVD → obstructive cause (tamponade or tension PTX)
• Cardiac tamponade: Beck's Triad → emergency pericardiocentesis
• Always use 5H/5T framework to drive targeted treatment during PEA
